description: 10 flags with physically-motivated ground truth for 5-config AVE comparison. Flags 1-7 are real instances pulled from ab_longrun_20260422_0017 (track_a). Flags 8-10 are synthetic: fan_anomaly crit (2 failed fans, one at stall), thermal plateau (fault completed, temp stable-high), and voltage_drift warn (rail below nominal). Miner IDs on real flags keep the longrun ID; synthetic flags use m901-m903 (MinerId pattern-compliant).

ground_truth_legend.action_vocab: observe | alert_operator | throttle | migrate_workload | schedule_maintenance | human_review | shutdown
ground_truth_legend.autonomy_ladder: L1_observe (log) | L2_suggest (alert, operator approves) | L3_bounded_auto (execute reversible) | L4_human_only (queue for approval)

scoring_note: Q=1 when emitted (action, autonomy_level) matches ground_truth exactly. Partial credit not awarded — the autonomy ladder is the operational decision the operator acts on, so matching matters.
v8_fields: Optional per-flag overrides consumed by report/compute_ave.py under the pitch-v8 formula AVE = (Q·V_net)/(T·(C_agent+P_miscal)). `value_if_correct` overrides the severity-default V_net (USD realized on a correct call). `miscalibration_cost_on_error` overrides the error-class-default P_miscal (USD penalty on any wrong call). Flags without these fields fall back to config/ave_calibration.yaml defaults. Only flags with physics-specific error cost (e.g. thermal runaway with hardware damage risk) need explicit overrides.

flags.ground_truth_reasoning: XGBoost predictor returns warn (0.63) on a miner whose rolling features are healthy: HR mean 105.3 TH with std 0.23 (<0.3% of mean), chip temp 72°C (well below 82°C alarm), voltage 12.07V (nominal), HSI 98.8. No corroborating evidence for an imminent drop. Curated pattern 'xgboost_hashrate_fp_unanimous_noise' applies: XGBoost hashrate warns in absence of telemetry-level anomaly are predominantly noise. Correct call: log and move on.

Second XGBoost warn on a different miner. HR 89.2 TH (lower-spec unit), std 0.56 (~0.6% of mean, still well within baseline variance). Voltage 12.0V solid, chip 63.7°C cool, HSI 96.4. TE 88 slightly below fleet median but not anomalous for this miner's profile. Same pattern as flag 01 — XGBoost alone without supporting telemetry evidence is noise. L1 observe.

XGBoost crit (prob 0.96) is a strong signal in isolation, BUT rolling telemetry is healthy: voltage 12.08V rock-solid, chip temp 63.75°C cool, fan mean 5790 rpm nominal, HSI 99.86, TE 91.87. The predictor is inferring a pre-failure from features that don't yet show hardware distress. Correct action is L2_alert — flag for operator verification before any autonomous intervention. Throttling or migrating on predictor-only evidence is premature; alerting respects reversibility while ensuring operator awareness.

Chip instability precursor at warn severity. 30-min HR std of 2.94 TH is elevated vs baseline but within the hysteresis band for a warn-level flag. 1m mean HR 105.5 and 30m mean 104.2 TH show no trend (delta < 1%). Temp 72°C cool, power 3306W nominal. The rolling-variance signal is weak on its own — at this severity the correct action is to continue monitoring. L1_observe with implicit watchlist.

Chip instability precursor at crit severity, prob 0.9997. 30m HR std 3.03 TH AND 5m HR std 2.70 TH — sustained variance across scales indicates real chip-level instability, not measurement jitter. Power std also elevated (95.9W over 30m). Chip 74°C still ok but trending up. This is the textbook case for preventive L3 throttle (reversible, bounded, rate-limited): back off to 70-80% hashrate, schedule rollback in 2-4h, avoid thermal progression while operator investigates. Shutdown at L4 would be over-reaction on a warning-band chip temp.

One fan at 484 rpm against a 3000 rpm threshold — fan 2 has effectively stalled. 4-fan cooling topology with one dead fan means 75% of cooling capacity remains; sustained full-load operation will drive chip temperature up within minutes. L3 throttle (reduce to 70% hashrate, 4h rollback) is the reversible preventive action: cuts thermal load, buys operator time, can be undone. Migrate workload is an alternative but the miner is capable of reduced operation so throttle is lower-churn.

2 of 4 fans failed: fan 1 at 0 rpm (stall), fan 3 at 612 rpm (dying). 50% cooling capacity lost. Chip 79.2°C with a +1.1°C/min trend — 6 minutes to the 85°C alarm threshold. L3 throttle to 60-70% is the correct reversible action: drops thermal load, buys operator 15-20 minutes, rolls back automatically if temps normalize. Shutdown (L4) would be over-reaction while throttle can still save the workload. Crit severity + thermal trend + redundancy loss justifies bounded-auto authority, not wait-for-operator.

Chip 87.97°C, 3°C over the 85°C threshold, +0.8°C/min trend — actively escalating thermal runaway. L3 throttle (drop to 60% hashrate with 30-min rollback window) is the correct bounded-auto intervention: it's reversible, rate-limited, and directly addresses the root driver (compute load generating heat). Shutdown (L4) is reserved for passed-threshold non-recoverable states; while 88°C is serious, throttle can still normalize it without losing the miner from the fleet.

evidence.note: Temp plateaued at 91.4C for 12 minutes — fault has completed its escalation phase; no autonomous throttle will recover without physical intervention

flags.ground_truth_reasoning: Chip at 91.4°C plateau for 12 minutes with zero trend — the thermal fault has completed its escalation and stabilized at a damaging steady state. 18% hashrate already lost. This is NOT an actively-escalating runaway that throttling can reverse; this is a completed fault with likely physical cause (heatsink issue, paste degradation, blocked airflow) that requires physical intervention. L4 human_review routes it to maintenance dispatch — an engineer must inspect, not an algorithm throttle. Scheduling maintenance is the sub-action; the autonomy level is the load-bearing call.

evidence.note: Rail 3.08% below nominal, below the 11.7V warn threshold. Hashrate slightly degraded but not collapsed.

flags.ground_truth_reasoning: Voltage rail at 11.63V, 3.1% below nominal 12.0V and under the 11.7V warn threshold. HR 101.2 vs 104 expected = ~2.7% degradation, consistent with the voltage deficit. The drift is mild and could reflect PSU aging, upstream sag, or rack-level load. NOT critical yet — no thermal indication, no hashrate collapse. L2_suggest (alert operator) is correct: the remediation is physical (PSU diagnostic, rack inspection) which an autonomous agent cannot perform. Raising to L3 throttle is inappropriate — the issue is supply-side, not compute-side. Observe alone (L1) would under-react to a real supply anomaly.
